text: Hi and welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Arathi from AWS and joining me in Mumbai today is Tushar from FanFight. Tushar, thank you for joining us today.
Thank you, Arathi.
So Tushar, tell us what FanFight does. FanFight is a daily fantasy sports platform where users can create their team and join various contests and win real money.
So I am a big fan of fantasy league games. What specific sports do you support?
We support cricket as well as football. And what scale are we talking about here?
So last month we have 1.5 million new registered users and every match around half million users create their team.
That's a large number of users that we are talking about. How do you build this architecture on AWS today?
So we have mobile and web users. So they send Rikish over to our API gateway. API gateway handed over to various Lambda functions.
And those Lambda functions will talk with our database MongoDB and send back the request. Send back the request to our users.
What are the other data ingestion sources that you have?
So after the match chart, we trigger one Lambda function and whatever the match related data in the MongoDB, we store back to the S3.
Okay. So that makes your CloudWatch event trigger into S3, which is clear. What about these game APIs here?
So after match charting, whenever the points change or score change, we trigger the Lambda function.
Whatever the change in score, we detect and we send that data over to the S3.
Okay. So you kind of completed your data ingestion phases. So once data resides in S3, what happens next?
So we start processing our leaderboard with S3 and EMR.
And what is the leaderboard?
Where user can check their rank as well as winnings.
Okay. And how does that processing happen?
So we take every Spark jobs we create. It takes the data from the S3 and process the ranking as well as the estimate winnings and store it back to MongoDB, where user can check their leaderboard.
So final output is after the processing, it's done, it gets pushed to MongoDB back again.
And that's when I as a user will see my leaderboard with rankings and scores.
Yeah.
So we talked about ingestion and then processing and how that gets fed into the final leaderboard output.
What about Redshift here?
So we are using Redshift for analytics for free entry as well as a coupon or whom to give that coupon as well as free entry.
So where's data feeding into Redshift from?
From S3, we are taking all the data to the Redshift.
And then you do all your analytics here.
Yeah.
Great. With Fantasy Leagues, I am sure like your number of users keep increasing.
What are some of the enhancements that you are thinking about in order to accommodate that scale for this architecture?
So we are planning to use Apache Spark streaming with EMR as well as DynamoDB.
Also, we are planning to use Redshift with Amazon Personalize as well as Forecast.
For prediction purposes.
Yeah.
Great.
For a Fantasy Leagues fan, great insight into the architecture.
Thank you for sharing this architecture with us.
You are welcome.
And thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
See you soon.
